does: This paper addresses the problem of redundant tool calls in LLM-based agents trained via reinforcement learning, where models lose sight of when to use external tools versus rely on parametric knowledge. The authors propose AKBE, an on-policy training method that simultaneously samples two trajectory rollouts per question—one with tool access and one without—to dynamically probe each instance's knowledge boundary. By comparing correctness outcomes across these dual paths, AKBE classifies trajectories into categories and generates fine-grained supervisory signals that teach the model efficient tool-use patterns. Rather than crude reward penalties for tool use, this approach provides targeted guidance that distinguishes when tools are genuinely necessary from when they represent wasteful redundancy, enabling more efficient agentic RL training with reduced computational overhead and improved decision boundaries.
connects: This work extends the line of research on agentic reinforcement learning for tool-using language models, building on prior work that trains agents to select and invoke external tools effectively. It responds to known limitations in reward-shaping approaches, which struggle to distinguish between legitimate tool requirements and inefficient over-reliance on tools, leading to optimization artifacts like reward hacking. The paper engages with the broader challenge of training language models to calibrate their confidence in parametric knowledge versus external information retrieval, a problem explored in contexts like in-context learning and factual grounding. It also relates to curriculum learning and fine-grained reward design in RL, drawing on techniques for constructing nuanced learning signals rather than coarse penalties.
enables: By establishing methods to cleanly separate when tools are necessary from when they introduce redundancy, AKBE enables more sample-efficient and cost-effective training of agentic systems. This capability points toward practical applications where tool use is expensive—such as API calls, external search, or computation—making efficient tool selection crucial. The approach of dynamically probing knowledge boundaries during training could extend to other domains requiring selective use of external capabilities, such as reasoning chains or retrieval-augmented generation systems. It also opens pathways for analyzing and improving model calibration, understanding where parametric knowledge is reliable, and designing agents that make transparent trade-offs between computation cost and answer quality, ultimately contributing to more robust and interpretable autonomous systems.
